mnemonics: I/O Methods MAP: Memory-mapped, Isolated I/O (Port-mapped), Polling, Interrupt-driven, DMA
keyPoints: Memory-mapped I/O uses the same address space for memory and devices, while isolated I/O uses a separate address space.
Programmed I/O (Polling) requires the CPU to constantly check device status, which is inefficient.
Interrupt-driven I/O allows devices to signal the CPU when they need service, freeing the CPU for other tasks.
Direct Memory Access (DMA) allows devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without continuous CPU involvement.
commonMistakes: Confusing Memory-mapped I/O with Isolated I/O (Port-mapped I/O) and their address space implications.
Assuming Polling is more efficient than Interrupt-driven I/O for most scenarios.
Forgetting that DMA requires a separate controller (DMAC) to manage the data transfers.
